policy_statement: Use the City Streamside Design Guidelines for recommendations on floodplain treatments, vegetation management, and streambank stabilization to mitigate flood events.
mechanism_description: Referring to streamside design guidelines to implement floodplain treatments, vegetation management, and streambank stabilization helps reduce flood impacts during flood events.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding) → ↓ exposure/sensitivity via floodplain/streambank measures → ↓ flood damages and disruption
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in system/activity: set or encourage use of streamside design guidelines that lead to floodplain treatments, riparian vegetation management, and streambank stabilization/restoration. (2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Increased flood storage/attenuation on floodplains, improved hydraulic roughness and infiltration, reduced bank erosion and channel instability, and moderated peak flows/velocities locally. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced flood risk and impacts (inundation extent/severity, erosion, infrastructure damage, service disruption), improving resilience to more intense/variable precipitation and runoff.
dominant_pathway_test: By improving streambank and floodplain condition, the biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood hazard impacts (lower inundation/erosion damages) during high-flow events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: flood hazard management—measures change river/floodplain form and function to attenuate flooding and erosion, yielding reduced impacts. 2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: while riparian restoration can add carbon sinks and improve water quality (Nature-Based Solutions co-benefits) and may reduce reconstruction resource use (Resource Efficiency co-benefit), the stated intent and main effect is risk reduction during flood events. 3) Edge cases applied: if guidelines are implemented purely as habitat/carbon projects with no material flood-risk objective, NBS might dominate; however this canonical mechanism explicitly frames outcomes as flood mitigation/impact reduction, so Adaptation remains primary. 4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy using “streambank/floodplain restoration guidelines → flood risk reduction” has the same primary risk-reduction pathway regardless of jurisdiction. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., NBS co-benefits, water quality, biodiversity) is deferred to policy-level evidence and is not set at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations are strongly nature-based (riparian reforestation, floodplain reconnection) and can deliver meaningful carbon sequestration; nonetheless, under this mechanism the dominant intended climate effect is flood-impact reduction (adaptation). If a specific policy centers carbon storage as the main objective/metric, reclassification at the policy level may be warranted.
key_indicators: “Refer to the City of Colorado Springs Streamside Design Guidelines”; “specific recommendations”; “floodplain treatments”; “vegetation management”; “stream bank stabilization”; “mitigate flood events”
co_benefits: Water quality improvement; habitat/riparian ecosystem enhancement; reduced sedimentation/erosion; recreational/amenity benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a design standard if incorporated into permitting or development review as a requirement; however, the verb “refer to” signals non-binding guidance (enabling) rather than a mandate.
